nodes.label: Ash Framework Core Philosophy
nodes.rationale: Ash is an opinionated, composable framework with a declarative approach to modeling domains, resources at the center. Do not assume prior knowledge of the framework or its conventions; read documentation before attempting to use its features.

nodes.label: require_atomic? false
nodes.rationale: Disables the default requirement that update/destroy changes and validations support atomic DB execution. Should be used sparingly — only when before_action/around_action hooks must read or modify the record, or a change reads current state via get_data/2 that can't be rewritten atomically. Not needed for simple attribute transforms, timestamps, counters, or after-action/after-transaction hooks; check whether atomic/3 callbacks can avoid it first.

nodes.label: Custom Modules vs Anonymous Functions
nodes.rationale: Prefer putting change/validation/preparation logic in its own named module rather than inline anonymous functions, for reuse, testability, and clarity.

nodes.label: Multiple Repos / Read Replicas
nodes.rationale: Configure separate repos per operation type (:read vs :mutate) so reads can be routed to a replica and writes to the primary, for scaling read-heavy workloads.

nodes.label: UserIdentity Resource
nodes.rationale: Stores the OAuth2/OIDC provider's iss/sub identity claims; matching users by email alone is unsafe. A new sub only auto-links to an existing account by email when the provider's email_verified claim is trusted (trust_email_verified?, default true for GitHub/Google/Auth0/Slack/Apple, false elsewhere). on_untrusted_email_match defaults to :reject; :confirm requires the confirmation add-on and must make clear in the confirmation email which provider is being linked, to prevent social-engineering account takeover.

nodes.label: Policy Check Evaluation Logic
nodes.rationale: Within a policy, the first check that yields a decision determines the outcome, so consecutive authorize_if calls behave as OR logic, not AND. To require multiple conditions together, use forbid_unless for the required condition before an authorize_if, combine conditions in one expr(cond1 and cond2), or split into separate policies that must each independently pass.

nodes.label: mix ash.codegen Workflow
nodes.rationale: Run mix ash.codegen with a descriptive name after each meaningful set of resource changes, to keep generated migrations organized and traceable to the change that produced them.

nodes.label: Code Organized Around Domains and Resources
nodes.rationale: Business logic belongs inside actions, not external modules; organize code around domains and resources, keeping each resource focused and well-named, and prefer domain-specific actions over generic CRUD operations.
nodes.norm_label: code organized around domains and resources

nodes.label: Form errors only display when protocol + field implemented
nodes.norm_label: form errors only display when protocol + field implemented

nodes.label: FK on_delete/on_update bypass resource logic (DB-level only)
nodes.norm_label: fk on_delete/on_update bypass resource logic (db-level only)

nodes.label: Positional form args exclude fields the form should not edit
nodes.norm_label: positional form args exclude fields the form should not edit

nodes.label: --dev migration workflow avoids premature migration naming
nodes.norm_label: --dev migration workflow avoids premature migration naming

nodes.label: require Ash.Query needed since filter/2 is a macro
nodes.norm_label: require ash.query needed since filter/2 is a macro

nodes.label: Globally unique identity values prevent concurrent test deadlocks
nodes.norm_label: globally unique identity values prevent concurrent test deadlocks

nodes.label: JS and CSS Guidelines (Tailwind v4 import syntax)
nodes.norm_label: js and css guidelines (tailwind v4 import syntax)

nodes.label: Matwork Project Overview & Design-Doc Authority Rule
nodes.rationale: docs/design.md is declared the authoritative spec for the platform; it must be read before any multi-file change, and if code and the design doc disagree the agent must say so rather than silently picking one.
nodes.norm_label: matwork project overview & design-doc authority rule

nodes.label: Tenancy & Authorization Iron Rules
nodes.rationale: Global resources are limited to User, Token, Gym, WebhookEvent; everything else must be attribute-multitenant on gym_id. Every Ash call must pass actor: and tenant:; authorize?: false is banned outside seeds/migrations/system-actor webhook jobs and must be flagged in diffs. Authorization must live in resource policies, never LiveViews/controllers, so LiveViews stay thin callers of domain code interfaces. Mux playback JWTs may only be minted inside Lesson.request_playback_token so no other code path can produce a watchable URL.

nodes.label: Money & External Services Iron Rules
nodes.rationale: Money is stored as integer cents only (never floats/Decimal). All Stripe/Mux calls must go through the Platform.Stripe/Platform.Mux behaviours, never direct HTTP/SDK calls elsewhere. Webhooks must be signature-verified, inserted as an idempotent WebhookEvent, then processed by an Oban job invoking a named Ash action with the system actor -- never processed inline in the controller. Subscription.status is a read-only mirror of Stripe state; only webhook/reconciliation actions may write it.

nodes.label: UI Components Iron Rule (StalwartUI)
nodes.rationale: Reusable components must live in lib/stalwart_ui/ and depend only on Phoenix.Component, Tailwind, and their own JS hooks -- never on resources, domains, or route helpers -- and take plain assigns. Any created/changed StalwartUI component must be reflected in COMPONENTS.md.

nodes.label: Testing Requirements (allow/deny policy paths, tenancy isolation)
nodes.rationale: Every policy must get tests for both allow AND deny paths, with the explicit framing that 'the deny paths are the product.' Tenancy isolation must be tested explicitly (a user in gym A must not read gym B's rows). Stripe/Mux use Mox against the Platform.* behaviours in unit tests, real test-mode keys only in dedicated integration tests. LiveView tests must cover invite-to-join and subscribe-to-watch.
nodes.norm_label: testing requirements (allow/deny policy paths, tenancy isolation)

nodes.label: usage_rules Mix Tool
nodes.rationale: A config-driven dev tool for Elixir projects that manages AGENTS.md files and agent skills sourced from dependencies, surfacing package-author-authored usage rules that should be thoroughly consulted before taking action.

nodes.label: BJJ Instructor Platform POC Design Document
nodes.norm_label: bjj instructor platform poc design document

nodes.label: User resource (global)
nodes.rationale: Kept global rather than tenant-scoped because identity is inherently global -- BJJ practitioners train at multiple academies, instructors guest-teach, and a gym owner may be a student elsewhere. Also sidesteps Ash's plug-ordering subtlety (tenant must be set before authentication plugs when the user resource itself is multitenant), since User isn't multitenant here.

nodes.label: Lesson resource (tenant-scoped)
nodes.rationale: Course -> section -> lesson is deliberately the shallowest hierarchy matching how instructors actually think (e.g. 'Half Guard: Fundamentals' -> 'Sweeps' -> a specific technique). free_preview lessons are watchable without payment as the standard, cheap-to-include conversion mechanic for content businesses. State transitions (publish, archive) are named Ash actions with policies, not generic updates.

nodes.label: Subscription resource (tenant-scoped)
nodes.rationale: Subscription.status is a mirror of Stripe's truth, written only by webhook-driven actions; authorization policies read the fast local row while a nightly Oban job reconciles against the Stripe API as a safety net. AshStateMachine for the subscription lifecycle was considered and explicitly skipped for the POC because a webhook-mirrored status atom is sufficient; revisit only if local state transitions multiply.

nodes.label: WebhookEvent resource (global)
nodes.rationale: Both Stripe and Mux webhooks are recorded first (idempotent on provider + external_id) and processed by Oban jobs second, which eliminates the entire 'we missed/double-processed a webhook' bug class and provides a free replay mechanism.

nodes.label: Ash Framework adoption rationale
nodes.rationale: Chosen for this app specifically because: (a) Ash enforces tenant isolation as a framework guarantee rather than relying on discipline -- any operation on a tenant-scoped resource without a tenant set is an error, not a silent cross-tenant leak, which matters for a platform whose pitch is 'your students and your revenue are yours'; (b) authorization lives as policies on resources rather than as a function someone must remember to call, making the content-gating rule structurally unavoidable; (c) AshJsonApi/AshGraphql can later derive a full API from the same resource definitions, nearly zeroing the cost of a future native-mobile client; (d) the author's prior Ash experience removes the learning-curve tax that would otherwise argue for vanilla Phoenix. Trade-off accepted: Stripe Connect and Mux integration work is plain Elixir regardless (Ash neither helps nor hurts there), and the codebase becomes legible to a smaller, more specialized pool of future collaborators -- though Ash-capable consulting is itself a scarcer, advertisable skill.

nodes.label: Attribute-strategy multitenancy
nodes.rationale: Single-database attribute-strategy multitenancy (gym_id column + Ash multitenancy block) chosen over schema-per-tenant (Ash's context strategy) because schema-per-tenant is rejected for its operational cost at this stage; the attribute strategy is also the path Alembic and the Ash docs recommend starting from, graduating later only if isolation demands it. Per-tenant identities also give useful uniqueness for free (e.g. StripeCustomer unique on user_id per gym without composite-index hand-wiring).

nodes.label: Stripe Connect design (Express, destination charges)
nodes.rationale: Express connected accounts with destination charges (Checkout Sessions created on the platform account, application_fee_percent, transfer_data.destination pointing at the gym's connected account) is the simplest Connect topology matching a flat SaaS fee + percentage-of-instructor-earnings pricing model. Stripe's hosted onboarding fully owns KYC/tax/payout schedules, which is exactly right for a solo platform operator.

nodes.label: Mux video design (direct upload, signed playback)
nodes.rationale: Video bytes never touch the app server (direct browser upload to Mux). Playback uses signed playback IDs from the start rather than public playback IDs, because retrofitting signing after launch would require re-creating assets -- called out as one of the few 'do it properly in the POC' items. The server mints short-lived playback JWTs only through the policy-gated Lesson.request_playback_token action.
nodes.norm_label: mux video design (direct upload, signed playback)

nodes.label: Policy-based content-gating authorization
nodes.rationale: Where a v1 (vanilla-Phoenix) design had a can_watch?/2 function every caller had to remember to invoke, v2 puts the rule directly on Lesson's request_playback_token action as Ash policies (free_preview OR owner/instructor membership OR active subscription, with a past_due grace window against churn), so it is structurally unbypassable -- the Mux JWT is only ever minted inside that action. Every future revenue stream extends these policies rather than scattering new checks.

nodes.label: StalwartUI component library strategy
nodes.rationale: All reusable UI lives under lib/stalwart_ui/, depending only on Phoenix.Component, Tailwind, and its own JS hooks, and must never reference an app resource, domain, or route helper -- the discipline that makes later extraction into a standalone, monetizable component library real. The POC is treated as the first production pressure-test of that future library (video player, curriculum tree, paywall gate, Connect onboarding card, invite flow, roster table), tracked in a running COMPONENTS.md inventory.

nodes.label: Build sequence / milestone plan
nodes.rationale: Ordered so every milestone ends with something demoable and the riskiest integrations (Mux, Stripe Connect) are reached as early as possible; deploying to Fly happens in Milestone 0 because deploying week 1 is much cheaper than deploying week 5. Milestone 2 (Money/Connect) is the biggest risk concentration -- Connect has the most unfamiliar moving parts and Ash helps least there -- which is why it is sequenced immediately after video rather than last.

nodes.label: Deferred decisions (recorded)
nodes.rationale: Native mobile deferred in favor of PWA-quality responsive web, with full Ash adoption keeping the AshJsonApi/AshGraphql door open for a future native client. Subdomains/custom domains: schema space reserved (Gym.custom_domain), resolution-plug swap later. Per-course purchases, seminars, merch, belt tracking, affiliate licensing: all post-POC but compatible with the existing resource model. bigserial chosen over UUID for the POC since UUIDs add friction with zero POC benefit; revisit only if federation/import-export makes enumeration a concern. Schema-based tenancy remains an available documented Ash migration path if a future enterprise customer demands hard isolation.

nodes.label: Magic-link-only authentication
nodes.rationale: No password authentication at all -- magic links only for sign-up and sign-in alike, since Ash's magic-link registration upserts by email, making an invited student's first click and a returning user's login the same flow. AshAuthentication's require-interaction default (token consumed on button click, not page fetch) is kept to prevent email security scanners from burning links. This makes reliable transactional email delivery (Resend/Postmark via Swoosh) a launch requirement, not a nicety.
